en.extract:
The black-bellied cuckoo or black-bellied squirrel cuckoo (Piaya melanogaster) is a bird of the family Cuculidae found in the Amazon region. Even though this species has a wide distribution, little is known about its ecology and natural history. The word melanogaster means "black belly"; it has Greek roots, melas meaning "black" and gaster meaning "belly".


Description
The average length of adults is between 38 and 40.5 cm. The beak is of an intense purple/red color, the iris is dark red with a blue orbital skin and one yellow mole at the anterior side of each eye. The head is grey and contrasts with the ruffled back. The throat and chest are brown-reddish, cinnamon color and the belly and undertail coverts are black. The tail is black with conspicuous white stripes. The juveniles resemble adults.
P. melanogaster occurs within the range of the similar squirrel cuckoo (Piaya cayana). Black-bellied cuckoos are distinguished by a dark face and grey crown. The squirrel cuckoo has exposed yellow-greenish skin in the orbital area, the chest plumage is grey and it lacks the characteristic hood of the black-bellied cuckoo.


Song
Their characteristic song is "dyerií-dyu, dyerií-dyu, dyerií-dyu…" sometimes repeated for a few minutes. It is often difficult to trace their position as they typically remain motionless in the forest when singing. They also produce faint grunts.


Phylogeny
The genus Playa is considered paraphyletic. The little cuckoo was formerly considered a third member of the genus but has now been placed in the Coccycua.


Distribution
P. melanogaster is a low density species with an Amazonian distribution; it can be found in the upper parts of tropical rainforests and occasionally in savanna forests. It prefers altitudes up to 800 meters above sea level. Is a native species of southern and eastern Guyana, Surinam, French Guiana, eastern Venezuela, Northern Bolivia, Colombia, Ecuador, eastern Perú and Brazil. It is a permanent resident in its range.
In Guyana, the black-bellied cuckoo can be found in Kanashen. In Bolivia it can be found in the department of La Paz. In Colombia it is found from the south of Meta department, the northwest of Guainía department and in southern Vaupés department. In Ecuador it is found below 400 meter above sea level, is infrequently seen in the eastern lowlands. In Brasil this cuckoo is found in the Alta Floresta region at the north of Mato Grosso, in southeastern Amazonia.


Population status
This species occurs over a wide range and the population appears to be decreasing. Due to the difficulty of population estimation it has not yet met the threshold for vulnerable status and is currently listed as a species of least concern.


Habitat
Black-bellied cuckoos live in tropical rainforests, scrub and occasionally in wooded savannas. They do not frequent open areas, like the squirrel cuckoo does. The black-bellied cuckoo stays at upper levels of the forest. It is estimated that deforestation reduces suitable habitat within its range by about 10% every three generations (approximately 13 years).


Behavior
P. melanogaster prefers forest canopies but can be seen in tall shrubs, climbing on vines, jumping between branches or running between them, and flying in open spaces. Their wing beats are slow and shallow. Individuals are usually alone or in pairs.


Reproduction
The reproductive cycle of P. melanogaster is little known. There are records of individuals in reproductive condition during the month of April in the Upper Orinoco in Venezuela. In French Guiana nestlings have been seen being fed from July to November. They build their nests in trees and lay up to 2 eggs. The eggs are pure white. The lifespan is 4.2 years.


Feeding
Their diet is made up of large insects such as beetles, cycads, grasshoppers, ants and caterpillars (even those with sharp hairs). This species feeds in the forest canopy. They also eat small vertebrates such as lizards.


References

The '''black-bellied cuckoo''' or '''black-bellied squirrel cuckoo''' ('''''Piaya melanogaster''''') is a [[bird]] of the family [[Cuculidae]] found in the Amazon region.<ref name=":0">{{cite news|title=Aves del Ecuador: Guía de Campo. Volumen II|last1=Ridgely|first1=Robert|last2=Greenfield|first2=Peter|publisher=Quito: Fundación de Conservación Jocotoco|year=2006}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{cite news|title=Playa melanogaster|url=http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/species/factsheet/22684376|author=BirdLife International|publisher=IUCN Red List of Threatened Species. Version 2014.1|date=2012|access-date=28 September 2016|archive-date=5 March 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160305050357/http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/species/factsheet/22684376}}</ref><ref name=":2">{{cite news|title=Nombres en castellano de las aves del mundo recomendados por la SEO (4ª parte p. 94)|url=http://www.seo.org/wp-content/uploads/tmp/docs/vol_45_4_cuarto1.pdf|last1=Bernis|first1=F|last2=de Juana|first2=E|last3=del Hoyo|first3=J|journal=Ardeola  |volume=45 |issue=1|date=1998}}</ref> Even though this species has a wide distribution, little is known about its ecology and natural history.<ref name=":2" /> The word ''melanogaster'' means "black belly"; it has Greek roots, ''melas'' meaning "black" and ''gaster'' meaning "belly".<ref name=":3">{{cite news|title=Phylogeny of the cuckoo genus Coccyzus (Aves: Cuculidae): a test of monophyly.|last=Hughes|first=J|journal=Systematics and Biodiversity  |volume=4 |issue=4 |pages=483–488|date=2006}}</ref>

== Description ==
The average length of adults is between 38 and 40.5 cm. The beak is of an intense purple/red color, the iris is dark red with a blue orbital skin and one yellow mole at the anterior side of each eye. The head is grey and contrasts with the ruffled back. The throat and chest are brown-reddish, cinnamon color and the belly and undertail [[Covert feather|coverts]] are black. The tail is black with conspicuous white stripes.<ref name=":0" /> The juveniles resemble adults.<ref name=":4">{{cite news|title=Black-bellied Cuckoo (Piaya melanogaster) » Planet of Birds|url=http://www.planetofbirds.com/cuculiformes-cuculidae-black-bellied-cuckoo-piaya-melanogaster|access-date=2016-09-30|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161001222022/http://www.planetofbirds.com/cuculiformes-cuculidae-black-bellied-cuckoo-piaya-melanogaster|archive-date=1 October 2016}}</ref>

''P. melanogaster'' occurs within the range of the similar squirrel cuckoo (''Piaya cayana'').<ref>{{cite news|title=Overview – Black-bellied Cuckoo (''Piaya melanogaster'') – Neotropical Birds|url=https://neotropical.birds.cornell.edu/portal/species/overview?p_p_spp=201976|access-date=2016-09-30}}</ref> Black-bellied cuckoos are distinguished by a dark face and grey crown.<ref name=":0" /> The squirrel cuckoo has exposed yellow-greenish skin in the orbital area, the chest plumage is grey and it lacks the characteristic hood of the black-bellied cuckoo.<ref name=":5">{{cite news|title=Wiki Aves de Colombia – Universidad Icesi – Cali, Colombia {{!}} Cuco Buchinegro – Piaya melanogaster|url=http://www.icesi.edu.co/wiki_aves_colombia/tiki-index.php?page_ref_id=1678|access-date=2016-09-30}}</ref>

=== Song ===
Their characteristic song is "dyerií-dyu, dyerií-dyu, dyerií-dyu…" sometimes repeated for a few minutes. It is often difficult to trace their position as they typically remain motionless in the forest when singing. They also produce faint grunts.<ref name=":0" />

=== Phylogeny ===
The genus ''Playa'' is considered [[Paraphyly|paraphyletic]]. The little cuckoo was formerly considered a third member of the genus but has now been placed in the ''Coccycua''.<ref name=":3" />

== Distribution  ==

''P. melanogaster'' is a low density species with an Amazonian distribution; it can be found in the upper parts of tropical rainforests and occasionally in savanna forests.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://neotropical.birds.cornell.edu/portal/species/overview?p_p_spp=201976|title=Overview – Black-bellied Cuckoo (Piaya melanogaster) – Neotropical Birds|website=neotropical.birds.cornell.edu|access-date=2016-10-04}}</ref> It prefers altitudes up to 800 meters above sea level. Is a native species of southern and eastern [[Guyana]], [[Suriname|Surinam]], [[French Guiana]], eastern [[Venezuela]], Northern [[Bolivia]], [[Colombia]], [[Ecuador]], eastern [[Peru|Perú]] and [[Brazil]]. It is a permanent resident in its range.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":4" />

In Guyana, the black-bellied cuckoo can be found in [[Kanashen]].<ref>{{cite news|title=Preliminary Bird Species Checklist of the Konashen COCA, Southern Guyana.|last=O'Shea|first=B.j.}}</ref> In Bolivia it can be found in the department of La Paz.<ref>{{cite news|title=Bolivia has the opportunity to create the planet's richest park for terrestrial biota|last1=Remsen|first1=J|last2=Parker|first2=T|journal=Bird Conservation International |volume=5 |issue=2–3 |pages=181–199 |date=1995}}</ref> In Colombia it is found from the south of Meta department, the northwest of Guainía department and in southern Vaupés department.<ref name=":5" /> In Ecuador it is found below 400 meter above sea level, is infrequently seen in the eastern lowlands.<ref name=":0" /> In Brasil this cuckoo is found in the Alta Floresta region at the north of Mato Grosso, in southeastern Amazonia.<ref>{{cite news|title=Birding the Alta Floresta region, northerns Mato Grosso, Brazil.|last=Whitney|first=B|journal=Cotinga |volume=7 |pages=64–68}}</ref>

=== Population status ===
This species occurs over a wide range and the population appears to be decreasing. Due to the difficulty of population estimation it has not yet met the threshold for vulnerable status and is currently listed as a species of least concern.<ref name=":1" />

== Habitat ==
Black-bellied cuckoos live in [[tropical rainforest]]s, scrub and occasionally in wooded savannas.<ref name=":5" /> They do not frequent open areas, like the squirrel cuckoo does. The black-bellied cuckoo stays at upper levels of the forest.<ref name=":0" /> It is estimated that deforestation reduces suitable habitat within its range by about 10% every three generations (approximately 13 years).<ref name=":1" />

== Behavior ==
''P. melanogaster'' prefers forest canopies but can be seen in tall shrubs, climbing on vines, jumping between branches or running between them, and flying in open spaces. Their wing beats are slow and shallow. Individuals are usually alone or in pairs.<ref name=":5" />

=== Reproduction ===
The reproductive cycle of ''P. melanogaster'' is little known. There are records of individuals in reproductive condition during the month of April in the Upper Orinoco in Venezuela. In French Guiana nestlings have been seen being fed from July to November.<ref name=":6">{{cite news |title=Black-bellied Cuckoo (Playa melanogaster) |url=http://www.hbw.com/node/54904 |access-date=2016-09-30}}</ref> They build their nests in trees and lay up to 2 eggs.<ref name="Piaya genus of cuckoos">{{cite news |title=Piaya (genus of cuckoos) |url=https://www.beautyofbirds.com/piayacuckoos.html |access-date=2016-09-30}}</ref> The eggs are pure white.<ref name=":5" /> The lifespan is 4.2 years.<ref name=":1" />

=== Feeding ===
Their diet is made up of large [[insect]]s such as [[beetle]]s, [[Cicada|cycads]], [[grasshopper]]s, [[ant]]s and [[caterpillar]]s (even those with sharp hairs). This species feeds in the forest canopy.<ref name=":4" /><ref name=":5" /><ref name=":6" /> They also eat small vertebrates such as lizards.<ref name="Piaya genus of cuckoos"/>
